ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for Bak Kut Teh; FOODON_03311737 (pork rib soup) is the closest defensible parent category.
ontology.foodex2_description: Pork, cooked, with bone, in broth
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a dedicated Bak Kut Teh code; A0CKP (pork cooked with bone in broth) is the closest defensible parent.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept maps to Bak Kut Teh or pork rib herb soup at this specificity.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of Bak Kut Teh Short Ribs consists of approximately 450 g, comprising pork short ribs in herbal broth served in a bowl, as commonly portioned at Singaporean and Malaysian restaurants.

nutrient_source.title: Bak Kut Teh (Pork Rib Soup) — IMR Malaysia MyFCD / FAO/INFOODS SEA Food Composition Table, adapted for short rib cut
nutrient_source.organization: Institute for Medical Research Malaysia (IMR) MyFCD; FAO/INFOODS Southeast Asian Food Composition Table
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values are adapted from IMR Malaysia MyFCD Bak Kut Teh (pork rib soup) entry and cross-referenced with FAO/INFOODS SEA food composition data for pork rib soup. Short ribs have a slightly higher fat content than spare ribs; values adjusted accordingly. USDA FoodData Central pork rib data (SR Legacy) used to validate protein and fat ratios for the short rib cut.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values — pork and meat-based soups with minimal carbohydrate
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database; Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. Diabetes Care 2008
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Bak Kut Teh Short Ribs. GI estimated at 15 based on analogous low-carbohydrate meat-based soups from the University of Sydney GI Database. The dish contains approximately 1.4 g carbohydrate per 100 g, predominantly from trace amounts of soy sauce and spices; GL per 450 g serving = (15 × 6.3 g available carbohydrate) / 100 = 0.9. GI category: low.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content from soy sauce and salted broth (~420 mg sodium per 100 g; ~1890 mg per 450 g serving)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit Bak Kut Teh Short Ribs to occasional consumption and request reduced soy sauce and salt in the broth, as a single serving can contribute approximately 82% of the WHO recommended daily sodium limit of 2300 mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low carbohydrate content (~1.4 g per 100 g) and low glycemic index (~15)
type2_diabetes.guidance: Bak Kut Teh Short Ribs is suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes due to its very low carbohydrate content and low glycemic index; however, portion size should be moderated to manage total fat and caloric intake, and sodium-reduced versions are preferred to avoid compounding cardiovascular risk.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Moderate saturated fat (~2.6 g per 100 g) and high sodium (~420 mg per 100 g) from the broth and soy sauce
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should consume Bak Kut Teh Short Ribs in moderation, choosing lean rib portions and requesting low-sodium broth, as the combination of saturated fat and high sodium can adversely affect blood pressure and lipid profiles when consumed frequently.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Bak Kut Teh Short Ribs (肉骨茶小排骨) is a traditional Malaysian and Singaporean pork rib soup dish in which pork short ribs are slow-simmered for several hours in a deeply aromatic broth made from a blend of Chinese medicinal herbs and spices including white pepper, garlic, star anise, cinnamon, cloves, dang gui (angelica root), and yu zhu (Solomon's seal rhizome), seasoned with soy sauce and salt. The dish originates from the Hokkien and Teochew Chinese communities of the Klang Valley in Malaysia and has become a beloved staple across Singapore and Malaysia. The broth is rich, savoury, and slightly peppery with earthy herbal undertones, while the short ribs are tender and fall-off-the-bone after prolonged cooking. Nutritionally, the dish is a moderate source of protein (~9.8 g per 100 g) and fat (~7.2 g per 100 g), with very low carbohydrate content (~1.4 g per 100 g), making it suitable for low-carbohydrate dietary patterns. The glycemic index is estimated at approximately 15 (low), making it appropriate for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes, though the high sodium content (~420 mg per 100 g) warrants caution for those with hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The dish is typically served with steamed white rice, you tiao (fried dough fritters), and dark soy sauce with chilli for dipping. It is not halal due to the use of pork. A standard restaurant serving weighs approximately 450 g.

Can diabetics eat Bak Kut Teh?

confidence_reason: Nutrient values are adapted from IMR Malaysia MyFCD Bak Kut Teh entries and cross-referenced with FAO/INFOODS SEA and USDA SR Legacy pork rib data. No exact entry for Bak Kut Teh Short Ribs exists in a single approved primary source; values represent a defensible adaptation. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database for low-carbohydrate meat soups. Confidence is medium rather than high due to the adapted rather than exact source match.
notes: 1. food_group corrected from 'Beverages' (input error) to 'Meat and alternatives', as Bak Kut Teh Short Ribs is a pork rib soup dish, not a beverage. 2. Nutrient values adapted from IMR Malaysia MyFCD Bak Kut Teh (pork rib soup) and validated against FAO/INFOODS SEA and USDA SR Legacy pork rib data; short rib cut has slightly higher fat than spare rib. 3. vitamin_d_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the IMR MyFCD or FAO/INFOODS SEA source entries for this dish. 4. vitamin_e_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the IMR MyFCD or FAO/INFOODS SEA source entries for this dish. 5. selenium_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the IMR MyFCD or FAO/INFOODS SEA source entries for this dish. 6. GI is an analogous estimate of 15 based on low-carbohydrate meat-based soups from the University of Sydney GI Database; no direct GI measurement for Bak Kut Teh exists in the approved GI literature. 7. The dish is not halal due to pork content. 8. Sodium content is high; a 450 g serving provides approximately 1890 mg sodium, representing ~82% of the WHO 2300 mg daily limit. 9. Multilingual names provided for English, Chinese (simplified and traditional), Malay, Tamil, and Hindi as required for Singaporean/Malaysian cuisine classification.
